content.full_text:
California
FairPolitical
Practices Commission
November 4,
1992
Mr.
Gary M.
Johnson
389 Showered Avenue
McFarland,
CA
93250
Re:
Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No.
1-92-544
Dear Mr.
Johnson:
I
am writing to confirm our telephone conversation on
October 23,
1992, wherein we discused your request for advice,
Letter No.
1-92-544.
You have requested advice regarding your
duties as a councilmember under the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Your letter does not seek advice regarding a specific pending
governmental decision and your question is general in nature.
Ac
cordingly,
we treat your letter as a request for informal as
sistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2
You are a councilmember and the owner of Johnson’s Hometown
Hardware in the City of McFarland.
You are concerned about the
city purchasing goods and supplies from your hardware business.
You stated that such purchases would not be on a contract basis,
but that goods would be purchased as needed.
You also stated that
the monthly amount of purchases would be minimal.
I explained
that the mere purchase of goods does not constitute a conflict—of—
interest under the Act.
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3) .)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-544
Page
2
I informed you that under Section 87103 of the Act,
a
conflict—of-interest only arises if a public official has a
financial interest in a governmental decision and it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
ef—
fect on an economic interest, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally.
When goods are purchased, the city becomes
a source of income to you.
However,
when your source of income is
a public agency, the “public generally” exception may be ap
plicable, thus allowing you to participate in decisions affecting
the public agency.
(Sandronsky Advice Letter,
No.
1-91—435.)
I also advised you to consult with your city attorney or the
Attorney General’s office regarding other conflict-of—interest
provisions,
such as Government Code Section 1090.
I have enclosed an outline and pamphlet on conflicts of
interest, which your city attorney may copy for the other
councilmembers.
I have also enclosed Regulation 18703.5 and
Advice Letter No.
1-90-313,
which discusses this regulation.
When a specific decision arises,
you may contact us for
advice daily between 9:00 a.m.
and 12:00 noon
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ,Genera1 Counsel
By:
J:
Stecher
;el,
Legal Division
SH:JS:dl
Enclosures

sections.question: You have requested advice regarding your duties as a councilmember under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). You are a councilmember and the owner of Johnson's Hometown Hardware in the City of McFarland. You are concerned about the city purchasing goods and supplies from your hardware business. You stated that such purchases would not be on a contract basis, but that goods would be purchased as needed. You also stated that the monthly amount of purchases would be minimal.
sections.conclusion: I explained that the mere purchase of goods does not constitute a conflict-of-interest under the Act. I informed you that under Section 87103 of the Act, a conflict-of-interest only arises if a public official has a financial interest in a governmental decision and it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on an economic interest, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. When goods are purchased, the city becomes a source of income to you. However, when your source of income is a public agency, the "public generally" exception may be applicable, thus allowing you to participate in decisions affecting the public agency.
sections.question_synthetic: As a city councilmember who owns a hardware business, does the city purchasing goods and supplies from that business create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The mere purchase of goods from a councilmember's business does not constitute a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. The "public generally" exception may apply when a public agency is the source of income, potentially allowing participation in decisions affecting the public agency.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter confirming telephone conversation. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections with headers, but question and conclusion content extracted from body text. Document advises consulting city attorney/AG on other conflict provisions like Government Code Section 1090. Marked as informal assistance under Regulation 18329 with no immunity provided.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have requested advice regarding your duties as a councilmember under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). You are a councilmember and the owner of Johnson's Hometown Hardware in the City of McFarland. You are concerned about the city purchasing goods and supplies from your hardware business. You stated that such purchases would not be on a contract basis, but that goods would be purchased as needed. You also stated that the monthly amount of purchases would be minimal.

CONCLUSION: I explained that the mere purchase of goods does not constitute a conflict-of-interest under the Act. I informed you that under Section 87103 of the Act, a conflict-of-interest only arises if a public official has a financial interest in a governmental decision and it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on an economic interest, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. When goods are purchased, the city becomes a source of income to you. However, when your source of income is a public agency, the "public generally" exception may be applicable, thus allowing you to participate in decisions affecting the public agency.

You have requested advice regarding your duties as a councilmember under the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Your letter does not seek advice regarding a specific pending governmental decision and your question is general in nature. Ac cordingly, we treat your letter as a request for informal as sistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2 You are a councilmember and the owner of Johnson’s Hometown Hardware in the City of McFarland. You are concerned about the city purchasing goods and supplies from your hardware business. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c) (3) .) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1-92-544 Page 2 I informed you that under Section 87103 of the Act, a conflict—of-interest only arises if a public official has a financial interest in a governmental decision and it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef— fect on an economic interest, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. When goods are purchased, the city becomes a source of income to you. However, when your source of income is a public agency, the “public generally” exception may be ap plicable, thus allowing you to participate in decisions affecting the public agency. (Sandronsky Advice Letter, No. 1-91—435.) embedding.summary: FPPC provides informal advice to a McFarland city councilmember who owns a hardware business regarding potential conflicts of interest when the city purchases goods from his business. The FPPC explains that mere purchases do not constitute a conflict of interest, and the "public generally" exception may allow participation in related decisions.
